title: Denis Johnson's life was a mess. His prose, somehow, was immaculate. How to explain the distance between life and art?
description: Denis Johnson's life was a mess. His prose, somehow, was immaculate. How to explain the distance between life and art?
hook: Denis Johnson's life was chaos incarnate. His sentences were flawless. What does that gap between the man and the work actually tell us?

title: A nameless narrator attempts to understand Gertrude Stein in Deborah Levy's new novel
description: In 'My Year in Paris with Gertrude Stein,' the narrator, a writer, actually spends one month trying to understand Stein's genius, how she invented herself, and her relationship with Alice B. Toklas.
hook: A writer spends one month obsessing over Gertrude Stein's genius—how she reinvented herself, how she wrote, how she lived with Alice B. Toklas.

title: Point of No Return: Digital Nostalgia in Backrooms
description: Like the internet lore for which it is named, Backrooms (2026) encapsulates a paradox of embodiment and time. Kane Parsons’s feature film—an adaptation of his cult Yo
hook: The Backrooms tapped into something primal about liminal spaces and digital decay. Here's how artists are weaponizing nostalgia to unsettle us.

title: Librarians on Horseback
description: "The New Deal project that preserved Kentucky’s recipes."
hook: The Depression-era librarians who rode horses through Kentucky mountains to preserve recipes. Food, survival, and what we choose to document.

title: The Critic’s Loves
description:
Harold Bloom wrote and edited so many books that it’s hard to imagine how he found the time between them to write letters.

The post The Critic’s Loves appeared first on The Point Magazine.
hook: Harold Bloom wrote so relentlessly that his letters barely survived. A meditation on what a critic's real passion looks like on the page.
